title: Phase Field Simulation for Dendrite Growth in Energy Storage Batteries

MaterialSystem.evidence: In this paper the phase transition means the transformation from the electrolyte phase to the electrode phase, as shown in Fig. 1b.

To investigate the underlying mechanisms of lithium dendrite growth and explore effective suppression strategies, many researchers have employed phase field models to simulate the dendrite growth process in lithium metal batteries.
With the rise of solid-state metal batteries, a growing number of researchers have also applied phase field modeling to solid-state lithium metal batteries.

Phenomenon.description: Formation and evolution of metal dendrites on the electrode surface during charging and discharging in energy storage batteries.
Phenomenon.evidence: during the repeated charging and discharging processes of energy storage devices, the uneven deposition of metal ions leads to the generation of dendrites on the electrode surface.

Phenomenon.description: Electrochemical deposition of metal ions at the electrode/electrolyte interface, including interface evolution and morphological instability.
Phenomenon.evidence: In the process of using the phase field model to simulate metal deposition and dendrite growth in electrochemical systems, the ion concentration field and applied electric field are closely linked to the complex chemical reactions in dendrite growth.

Phenomenon.description: Formation of electrically isolated lithium during stripping and dendrite evolution, causing performance degradation.
Phenomenon.evidence: The phase-field method exhibits powerful analytical capabilities in the study of dead lithium by dynamically simulating lithium stripping and dendrite growth, thereby revealing the key factors contributing to dead lithium formation.

Phenomenon.description: Crack growth and interaction with dendrites in solid-state electrolytes under stress and dendrite penetration.
Phenomenon.evidence: These studies collectively indicate that: (1) electrolytes may crack and expand under dendritic stress, leading to material failure; (2) stress concentrations at crack tips and voids can alter dendrite morphology

Phenomenon.description: Modification of dendrite growth behavior by the solid electrolyte interphase through physical barrier effects, structural heterogeneity, and chemistry-dependent transport/mechanics.
Phenomenon.evidence: The solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), formed as a passivation layer at the interface between the electrolyte and the electrode, can significantly affect the growth of lithium dendrites.

Parameter.description: Order parameter describing the phase states of the system.
Parameter.evidence: In the phase field model, the basic field variables are the phase field variable ξα (α = 1,…,n), which describe the phase states of the system.

Parameter.description: Concentration variable for system components.
Parameter.evidence: the concentration variables ci(I = 1,…,m) are also introduced into the system.

Parameter.description: Interface mobility between phase α and phase β.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, Lαβ represents the interface mobility between phase α and phase β

Parameter.description: Atomic mobility between component i and component j.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, Mij is the atomic mobility between component i and component j.

Parameter.description: Chemical contribution to total free energy density.
Parameter.evidence: In the formula, fchem represents chemical energy

Parameter.description: Electrostatic contribution to total free energy density.
Parameter.evidence: felec represents electrostatic energy

Parameter.description: Elastic contribution to total free energy density.
Parameter.evidence: and felas represents elastic energy.

Parameter.description: Electric potential field in the electrochemical system.
Parameter.evidence: φ represents the electric potential.

Parameter.description: System temperature or ambient/electrolyte temperature.
Parameter.evidence: T represents the system temperature.

Parameter.description: Electrochemical overpotential driving electrodeposition and dendrite growth.
Parameter.evidence: In phase-field models, overpotential serves as one of the key variables in the control equations, acting as the primary driving force for dendrite growth.

Parameter.description: Mechanical stress or external pressure affecting dendrite growth.
Parameter.evidence: Most findings suggest that higher mechanical strength of the electrolyte and greater external stress exert a suppressive effect on dendrite growth

Parameter.description: Mechanical stiffness of electrolyte or SEI related to dendrite suppression.
Parameter.evidence: Most results indicate that a high Young’s modulus and external stress can effectively inhibit dendrite growth.

Parameter.description: Strength of surface-energy anisotropy.
Parameter.evidence: δ is the anisotropic strength.

Parameter.description: Pore size of porous separator.
Parameter.evidence: smaller pore sizes lead to more uniform lithium-ion concentrations on the electrode surface

Parameter.description: Thickness of separator.
Parameter.evidence: thicker separators result in more uniform dendrite growth, thereby reducing overall growth rates

Parameter.description: Lithium-ion or cation concentration field near the electrode.
Parameter.evidence: the ion concentration at the dendrite tip dynamically evolves during the growth process

Parameter.description: Rate of dendrite elongation or growth velocity.
Parameter.evidence: a linear relationship has also been observed between the cation concentration on the electrode surface and the dendrite growth rate.

Parameter.description: Degree of side branching in dendrite morphology.
Parameter.evidence: as the overpotential increases, the dendrite growth rate accelerates significantly... the instability of the electrodeposition process is also intensified, leading to more pronounced dendritic branching

Parameter.description: Qualitative morphology of dendrites such as mossy, tree-like, fibrous, spherical, or needle-like.
Parameter.evidence: the electric field leads to a morphological evolution from mossy structures (initially also needle-like or fibrous) to tree-like structures with increased branching as the applied voltage increases.

Parameter.description: Residual or accumulated amount of dead lithium.
Parameter.evidence: the relationship between residual dead lithium and discharge voltage/current is non-monotonic

Parameter.description: Available active lithium surface area in the battery.
Parameter.evidence: The presence of dead lithium directly reduces the active lithium surface area

Parameter.description: Battery polarization or overpotential increase associated with dead lithium.
Parameter.evidence: increases overpotential/battery polarization

Parameter.description: Loss of battery capacity.
Parameter.evidence: and leads to capacity loss

Parameter.description: Applied voltage in dendrite simulations or experiments.
Parameter.evidence: the electric field leads to a morphological evolution from mossy structures (initially also needle-like or fibrous) to tree-like structures with increased branching as the applied voltage increases.

Parameter.description: Applied current density affecting deposition and dendrite growth.
Parameter.evidence: under appropriate current density conditions, can effectively hinder dendrite propagation.

Assumption.statement: The electrode-electrolyte interface is treated as a transition region rather than a sharp interface.
Assumption.evidence: During dendrite growth, the electrode–electrolyte interface (EEI) surface does not exhibit a simply sharp interface but rather features a distinct transition region.

Assumption.statement: Phase variables are treated as non-conserved variables governed by the Allen-Cahn equation.
Assumption.evidence: Phases are usually regarded as non-conserved variables, and their evolution follows the Allen-Cahn equation

Assumption.statement: Concentration variables are conserved and follow the Cahn-Hilliard equation when there is no chemical reaction.
Assumption.evidence: If there is no chemical reaction, the concentration variable are conserved variables and follows the Cahn-Hilliard equation

Assumption.statement: Guyer's phase-field control equation for the phase field variable is derived from the thermodynamic framework of an ideal solution.
Assumption.evidence: the control equation for the phase field variable (Eq. 4) is derived from the thermodynamic framework of an ideal solution.

Assumption.statement: The ion diffusion equation adopts a simple linear kinetic assumption.
Assumption.evidence: The ion diffusion equation (Eq. 5) adopts a simple linear kinetic assumption, ignoring the complex interactions between ions.

Assumption.statement: Charge distribution at the electrode/electrolyte interface is treated as uniform in the simplified model.
Assumption.evidence: For the charge distribution at the electrode/electrolyte interface, an idealized treatment is adopted, considering the charge distribution to be uniform.

Assumption.statement: The electrolyte and anode are considered as linear elastic materials in the coupled mechanical model.
Assumption.evidence: Considering the electrolyte and anode as linear elastic materials

Term.description: Interfacial gradient contribution controlling diffuse-interface evolution.
Term.evidence: Compared with earlier models, Eq. 11 represents a modified form of the Butler–Volmer formulation... The free energy component includes a gradient term

Term.description: Species diffusion contribution in transport equations.
Term.evidence: Equation 12 employs the Nernst–Planck equation in conjunction with the charge neutrality condition to describe the coupling between lithium-ion concentration and the electric field.

Term.description: Electromigration contribution coupling ion transport to electric field.
Term.evidence: Equation 12 employs the Nernst–Planck equation in conjunction with the charge neutrality condition to describe the coupling between lithium-ion concentration and the electric field.

Term.description: Electrochemical reaction contribution controlling interface motion.
Term.evidence: Eq. 11 represents a modified form of the Butler–Volmer formulation, incorporating driving forces from both free energy and electrochemical reactions.

Term.description: Noise term added to represent SEI influence in total free energy.
Term.evidence: A widely adopted approach in recent studies is to incorporate a noise term into the total free energy functional to implicitly represent the influence of the SEI on dendrite growth

Term.description: Elastic strain energy contribution for mechanical coupling.
Term.evidence: In 2018, Yurkiv36 proposed a method for calculating elastic strain energy (Eq. 15) and incorporated it into the total free energy functional.

ModelEquation.description: Evolution equation for non-conserved phase-field variables.
ModelEquation.evidence: Phases are usually regarded as non-conserved variables, and their evolution follows the Allen-Cahn equation

ModelEquation.description: Conserved evolution equation for component concentrations.
ModelEquation.evidence: If there is no chemical reaction, the concentration variable are conserved variables and follows the Cahn-Hilliard equation

ModelEquation.description: Total free-energy functional composed of chemical, electrostatic, elastic and additional energy terms.
ModelEquation.evidence: To solve the above equations, the total free energy of the system is expressed as an integral of energy terms, as shown in Eq. 3.

ModelEquation.description: Electrostatic balance relation used in simplified electrochemical phase-field models.
ModelEquation.evidence: The Poisson equation (Eq. 6) is then used to maintain potential balance.

ModelEquation.expression: Phase-field evolution equation combining gradient/free-energy driving force with Butler-Volmer electrochemical reaction kinetics
ModelEquation.description: Modified interface evolution law for dendrite growth under electrochemical driving.
ModelEquation.evidence: Eq. 11 represents a modified form of the Butler–Volmer formulation, incorporating driving forces from both free energy and electrochemical reactions.

ModelEquation.expression: Coupled ion transport equation using Nernst-Planck transport and charge neutrality
ModelEquation.description: Coupled transport relation for lithium-ion concentration and electric field.
ModelEquation.evidence: Equation 12 employs the Nernst–Planck equation in conjunction with the charge neutrality condition to describe the coupling between lithium-ion concentration and the electric field.

ModelEquation.description: Heat transfer equation used to couple temperature effects into phase-field simulations.
ModelEquation.evidence: In 2018, Yan et al.25 coupled Chen’s33 phase field model and heat transfer model (Eq. 14) through the diffusion coefficient.

ModelEquation.description: Mechanical equilibrium relation used to compute local stresses during dendrite growth.
ModelEquation.evidence: they solved the mechanical equilibrium equation (Eq. 16) and obtained the local stress tensor (Eq. 17).

ModelEquation.description: Surface-energy anisotropy relation used in phase-field models.
ModelEquation.evidence: adopted the formulation proposed in Ref. [89] (Eq. 20) to account for surface energy anisotropy

ModelEquation.description: Free-energy functional augmented by a noise term to represent SEI effects.
ModelEquation.evidence: A widely adopted approach in recent studies is to incorporate a noise term into the total free energy functional to implicitly represent the influence of the SEI on dendrite growth

SimulationModel.objective: Describe and analyze dendrite growth in energy storage batteries using the phase-field framework.
SimulationModel.evidence: The phase-field method is an effective numerical simulation approach capable of accurately describing the dynamic process of dendrite growth in energy storage batteries.

SimulationModel.objective: Couple phase field, ion concentration field, and electric potential field to simulate electrodeposition and dendrite growth.
SimulationModel.evidence: The phase field model usually couples the phase field, ion concentration field, and applied electric field together by using control equations.

SimulationModel.numerical_method: phase-field model coupled with heat transfer through diffusion coefficient
SimulationModel.objective: Study the influence of temperature field on dendrite growth.
SimulationModel.evidence: In 2018, Yan et al.25 coupled Chen’s33 phase field model and heat transfer model (Eq. 14) through the diffusion coefficient.

SimulationModel.objective: Analyze coupled electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical effects on dendrite growth and cracking in solid-state systems.
SimulationModel.evidence: developing multiphysics phase-field models that couple stress fields, thermal fields, and other physical phenomena.

SimulationModel.numerical_method: free-energy augmentation using noise term, current correction factor, or geometric reconstruction
SimulationModel.objective: Represent physical and chemical effects of the SEI on dendrite evolution.
SimulationModel.evidence: Some researchers have addressed the SEI’s role using methods such as current correction factors, geometric reconstruction, and the introduction of noise fields.

SimulationModel.objective: Account for anisotropy in surface energy and morphology evolution of dendrites.
SimulationModel.evidence: Anisotropy reflects the variation in a material’s physical properties along different crystallographic orientations, enabling more realistic simulation of dendritic behaviors such as branching and coarsening
